description: Consolidated Beyou backend service-health board — overview cards, JVM/system, memory & GC deep dives, HTTP server, HikariCP pool, Caffeine cache, Spring Data, Spring Security, scheduled tasks and diagnostics. Replaces the old per-area dashboards.

panels.title: Overview — how are we right now?

panels.description: Process CPU share for this JVM right now (avg across selected services). 100% = one full core busy; the JVM can exceed 100% across cores.
panels.title: CPU usage (now)

panels.description: JVM heap used now vs configured max (-Xmx). 'max' sums pools that report a real ceiling (some young-gen pools report -1 under G1).
panels.title: Heap (used / max)

panels.description: HikariCP Postgres pool: connections checked out now vs configured maximum. Active sustained near max = the pool is the bottleneck.
panels.title: DB connections (active / max)

panels.description: Server requests being processed right now (Micrometer LongTaskTimer). This is concurrency — NOT Tomcat sessions (we use stateless bearer tokens). Reads 0 when idle (the active series only exists while a request runs).
panels.title: HTTP requests in flight

panels.description: Total HTTP server requests received over the dashboard's selected time range. Resizes when you change the time picker.
panels.title: HTTP requests (in window)

panels.description: JVM process CPU share and whole-host system CPU. Process near system = this JVM owns the load; system high but process low = a noisy neighbour.
panels.title: CPU usage (process vs system)

panels.description: OS 1-minute load average plus the CPU-count line. Load above CPU count = host oversubscribed; threads waiting for CPU.
panels.title: System load (1m) vs CPU count

panels.description: Total heap bytes in use per service. Per-pool breakdown is in Memory deep dive.
panels.title: Heap memory used

panels.description: Metaspace, code cache, compressed class space, compiler buffers. Steady growth = classloader leak; sudden jump = a deploy.
panels.title: Non-heap memory used

panels.description: Stop-the-world seconds per second of wall clock (smoothed 5m). 0.05 = 5% of the JVM was paused. Sustained > 0.10 hurts latency.
panels.title: GC pause time fraction

panels.description: Micrometer's GC overhead estimate (0-1): share of recent wall time the GC consumed. Sustained > 0.05 = GC is the bottleneck.
panels.title: GC overhead

panels.description: Live threads by state, stacked. RUNNABLE = CPU work; WAITING/TIMED_WAITING = idle/sleeping; BLOCKED = waiting on a monitor lock (concerning if sustained).
panels.title: Threads by state

panels.description: Logback emission rate per level (smoothed 5m). Spikes in ERROR/WARN are the first place to look during an incident.
panels.title: Log events / s by level

panels.description: Bytes/s allocated on the heap (young-gen pressure). High allocation forces frequent minor GCs even if the heap fits.
panels.title: Memory allocation rate

panels.description: Filesystem usage percent per monitored mountpoint. Watch sustained growth — log rotation gone wrong, runaway temp files.
panels.title: Disk usage

panels.description: Open FDs (sockets, files, pipes) vs the OS limit (ulimit -n). Climbing toward max = FD leak; sudden jump = a burst of connections.
panels.title: Open file descriptors

panels.description: Heap bytes used per pool, stacked. Under G1: Old Gen (long-lived), Eden (allocation buffer), Survivor. Growing Old Gen alone = leak; growing Eden + frequent minor GC = healthy churn.
panels.title: Heap by pool (id)

panels.description: Non-heap bytes per pool: Metaspace, Code Cache, Compressed Class Space, Compiler buffers. Stacked largest at bottom.
panels.title: Non-heap by pool (id)

panels.description: Heap that survives a full GC (live set) vs the max promoted region. Live trending up = real memory growth, not just allocation churn.
panels.title: Heap live data (post-GC)

panels.description: DirectByteBuffer usage (off-heap NIO). Steady growth here can OOM the container without showing up in heap.
panels.title: Direct buffer used

panels.description: Fraction of the long-lived pool occupied immediately after a GC. Trending up = the true working set is growing (not just churn).
panels.title: Memory usage after GC (long-lived)

panels.description: Worst single STW pause duration per GC cycle, per action. Spikes here become latency outliers in HTTP responses.
panels.title: GC max pause (upper) per cycle

panels.description: GC cycles per second by action (end of minor/major GC). Sustained high rate = young gen too small for the allocation rate, or memory pressure.
panels.title: GC cycles / s

panels.description: Stop-the-world seconds per second split by GC action. Lets you see whether minor or major collections dominate the pause budget.
panels.title: GC pause seconds / s by action

panels.description: Bytes/s promoted from young to old gen. High promotion = objects living long enough to survive several minor GCs — big allocations, caches, session data.
panels.title: Memory promoted rate (-> old gen)

panels.description: Cumulative ms in the JIT compiler (C1/C2). Should plateau a few minutes after startup. Sustained growth post-warmup = JIT thrashing.
panels.title: JIT compilation time (cumulative)

panels.description: Inbound HTTP request rate grouped by status code, stacked. Usually 200, then 4xx, then 5xx.
panels.title: Request rate (5m) by status

panels.description: Same as by-status but grouped by HTTP method. Useful to spot a sudden spike of writes.
panels.title: Request rate (5m) by method

panels.description: Server-side latency percentiles computed from the native histogram buckets, per URI. Compare p50 (typical user) vs p95/p99 (slow tail).
panels.title: HTTP latency by URI (p50 / p95 / p99)

panels.description: Fraction of requests returning 4xx and 5xx. 5xx is server fault; 4xx is usually client/auth. Numerator guarded with 'or vector(0)' so it reads 0 (not 'no data') when there are zero errors but traffic is flowing.
panels.title: Error rate (4xx / 5xx share)

panels.description: Server requests processed simultaneously, per service. Sustained high = request rate exceeds per-request latency capacity — scale out or speed up. Flat 0 when idle.
panels.title: Concurrent requests in flight

panels.description: Inbound request rate per endpoint. The busiest URIs are where optimisation and caching pay off most.
panels.title: Request rate (5m) by URI

panels.description: Top 20 (uri, method, status) tuples by request count over the selected time range. Resizes with the time picker.
panels.title: Requests by endpoint (window total)

panels.description: Tabular endpoint breakdown — full URI text, sortable columns, exportable as CSV.
panels.title: Top URIs by request count (window total)

panels.description: Hikari connections per state: active (checked out), idle (in pool), pending (threads WAITING for a connection — saturation!), total.
panels.title: Connections by state

panels.description: Configured pool bounds (minimum-idle and maximum-pool-size). Compare to the active count on the left.
panels.title: Pool bounds (min / max)

panels.description: Time to obtain a connection from the pool. Climbing = pool saturation or Postgres slow. Sustained high = grow the pool or release connections faster.
panels.title: DB acquire latency (avg / max)

panels.description: Rate of connection check-outs from the pool. Each HTTP request that hits the DB typically acquires one.
panels.title: Acquire rate / s

panels.description: avg = average time a connection is held before return. timeouts/s = threads that gave up waiting (pool fully saturated past connectionTimeout).
panels.title: Connection hold time + timeouts

panels.description: Hit ratio per cache: hits / (hits + misses), smoothed 5m. Low or falling = cache thrash or cold cache after eviction.
panels.title: Cache hit rate (%) by cache

panels.description: Get rate split into hits and misses per cache. Lots of gets + few misses = healthy. Rising misses = working set outgrew the cache.
panels.title: Cache hits vs misses (/s)

panels.description: Overall hit ratio across all caches. Below ~0.5 means the cache is doing little useful work.
panels.title: Total cache hit rate (5m)

panels.description: Live entry count per cache. Flat at the configured maximum = cache is full and evicting to make room.
panels.title: Cache size (entries)

panels.description: Entries evicted per second per cache. A spike = bulk write triggered invalidation, or the cache is too small (size-based eviction).
panels.title: Cache evictions (/s)

panels.description: Entries written per second per cache. High puts with low hit rate = we keep filling the cache but rarely read before eviction.
panels.title: Cache puts (/s)

panels.description: Spring Data repository method invocations per repository over the selected time range. The hot repositories are your DB workload.
panels.title: Invocations (window total) by repository

panels.description: Per-repository method latency: avg = rate(sum)/rate(count); max = worst step. Spikes = lock contention, missing index, or cold cache.
panels.title: Repository latency (avg / max)

panels.description: Top 20 repository.method calls over the selected range. The biggest bars are your most-called queries.
panels.title: Top repository methods (window total)

panels.description: Same top-20 list as a table, with sortable columns including state (SUCCESS / ERROR).
panels.title: Repository invocations (window total)

panels.description: Authorization outcomes (true = GRANTED, false = DENIED) per second. Sustained DENIED = a client hitting endpoints it shouldn't, or a role change broke a user.
panels.title: Authorization decisions (5m)

panels.description: Requests that passed through Spring Security's filter chain. Should mirror inbound HTTP volume.
panels.title: HTTP secured requests (/s)

panels.description: Top 10 security filters by mean processing time. Most are sub-millisecond; the auth/bearer-token filters are usually the heaviest.
panels.title: Top filter chains by avg latency

panels.description: Rate of @Scheduled method executions (e.g. RoutineSnapshotScheduler). Empty until a scheduler fires after process start.
panels.title: Scheduled execution rate (/s)

panels.description: Average and worst execution time for @Scheduled jobs. A scheduler taking longer than its interval will overlap or fall behind.
panels.title: Scheduled execution duration (avg / max)

panels.description: Seconds since process start, per service. A drop to 0 = a restart. Sanity check that nothing crash-looped recently.
panels.title: Uptime

panels.description: Classes currently loaded vs cumulative unloaded. Sustained growth in loaded without matching unloaded = classloader leak.
panels.title: Classes loaded vs unloaded

panels.description: Live, daemon, and peak thread counts. Peak ratcheting up without coming back = a thread leak (often unbounded executors).
panels.title: Thread count (live / daemon / peak)

title: Beyou — Service Health (consolidated)
